name: The Barranca and Cerro del Castillo by Navacerrada – circular from Becerril de la Sierra
name_original: La Barranca y Cerro del Castillo por Navacerrada – circular desde Becerril de la Sierra
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Becerril De La Sierra are situated within the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park, characterized by a mountain climate and varied terrain. The region features prominent peaks, lush valleys, ancient forests, and distinctive granite formations. Notable water bodies include the Navacerrada Reservoir and the Arroyo de la Laguna. Best touring cycling routes around Becerril De La Sierra The most popular touring cycling route is The Barranca and Cerro del Castillo by Navacerrada – circular from Becerril de la Sierra, a 18.8 miles (30.2 km) trail that takes 2 hours 52 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the Valle de La Barranca and offers views of Cerro del Castillo. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Old Castle of Manzanares – San Isidro Hermitage loop from Becerril de la Sierra, a difficult 29.9 miles (48.1 km) path. This route passes by historical landmarks such as the Old Castle of Manzanares and the San Isidro Hermitage. Local touring cyclists also love the Manzanares el Real Castle – Batán Bridge loop from puente medieval del Batan, a 25.0 miles (40.3 km) trail leading through areas near the Manzanares el Real Castle, often completed in about 2 hours 27 minutes. Touring cycling around Becerril De La Sierra is defined by majestic peaks, verdant valleys, and impressive granite formations. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate paths to more challenging ascents. The routes in Becerril De La Sierra are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 220 reviews. More than 8000 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Becerril De La Sierra's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Becerril De La Sierra?
A: There are over 300 touring cycling routes available around Becerril De La Sierra, offering a wide range of options for different preferences and skill levels.

Q: What is the general difficulty level of touring cycling routes in Becerril De La Sierra?
A: The routes vary significantly in difficulty. While there are 32 easy routes and 109 moderate options, the majority, around 166, are classified as difficult. This means you can find paths suitable for a relaxed ride or a challenging ascent through the mountains.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, many routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. For example, you can explore &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e959822859/the-barranca-and-cerro-del-castillo-by-navacerrada-circular-from-becerril-de-la-sierra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Barranca and Cerro del Castillo by Navacerrada – circular from Becerril de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23833089" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Castle of Manzanares – San Isidro Hermitage loop from Becerril de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural features can I expect to see while touring cycling?
A: The region is rich in natural beauty. You'll encounter majestic peaks like La Maliciosa, lush valleys, ancient forests, and distinctive granite formations such as those found in La Pedriza. Notable water bodies include the Navacerrada Reservoir and the Arroyo de la Laguna. You can also visit the unique &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3692534" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñalara Glacial Cirque&lt;/a&gt; with its serene lagoons.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or cultural sites accessible by touring bike?
A: Absolutely. You can cycle past historical landmarks such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/641391" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Road of Puerto de la Fuenfría&lt;/a&gt;, which is the best-preserved Roman road section in the Community of Madrid. Other routes pass by the Old Castle of Manzanares, the San Isidro Hermitage, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2555399" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Batán Bridge&lt;/a&gt;. The Camino de Santiago de Madrid also traverses the municipality.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Becerril De La Sierra?
A: The routes in Becerril De La Sierra are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 220 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the stunning mountain views, and the opportunity to explore both natural landscapes and historical sites.

Q: When is the best time of year for touring cycling in Becerril De La Sierra?
A: Given its mountain climate, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for touring cycling, with milder temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summer can be warm, especially at lower elevations, while winter may bring colder temperatures and snow to higher peaks, making some routes more challenging or inaccessible.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in Becerril De La Sierra?
A: Yes, there are 32 easy routes that are generally more suitable for families or those looking for a less strenuous ride. These routes typically feature gentler gradients and less technical terrain, allowing for a more relaxed experience.

Q: Are there routes that offer panoramic views or viewpoints?
A: Many routes in the Sierra de Guadarrama offer breathtaking panoramic views. The region is known for its majestic peaks and significant mountain passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4225665" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Navacerrada Pass&lt;/a&gt;. The Cerro del Hilo viewpoint provides stunning vistas of the National Park, and routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38291158" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto del Enebrillo Viewpoint – Views of La Pedriza loop from Mataelpino&lt;/a&gt; are specifically designed to showcase scenic overlooks.

Q: Can I find routes that pass by reservoirs or lakes?
A: Yes, the area features several picturesque water bodies. The Navacerrada Reservoir is a significant local landmark, and you can find routes that pass by it. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31548137" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Manzanares el Real Castle – Santillana Reservoir loop from Becerril de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt; is a great option to cycle alongside the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2712180" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santillana Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the touring cycling routes?
A: The terrain is varied, ranging from paved roads suitable for road bikes to comprehensive mountain biking (BTT) routes. You'll encounter everything from challenging ascents on mountain passes to paths through lush valleys, ancient forests, and areas with distinctive granite formations like La Pedriza.
tips:
The Las Canchas viewpoint is an extraordinary panoramic point of the Barranca Valley, enclosed by the Guarramillas and Maliciosa massifs. The enigmatic and mysterious name of Maliciosa appears on several occasions in local chronicles from the fourteenth century and is usually related to tragic events that occurred to countrymen from nearby towns. The best known were the murders of the bandit Pablo Santos, in the Peña del Mediodía; of the shepherd nicknamed El Mirlo, on the Valdehalcones pass; and of another shepherd with an unknown name who died in the Peña de la Barranca.

Looking face to face at the mountain from the southern slope, it is easy to agree with the shepherds of yesteryear and “like Madaleta in the Pyrenees, Maliciosa is in Guadarrama, the cursed one; land of the most mistreated in the Sierra, where the rock, always desolate and naked, which time continuously sharpens and wears away, dies in its beauty, deeper and deeper”. The Pedriza del Real de Manzanares. Constancio Bernaldo de Quiros. Uneven Editions 1999.

One of the best known towns in the Sierra de Madrid, possibly for giving its name to a reservoir, a port and a ski resort. Its name comes from the root nava, which meant "flat and floodable land between mountains" so perhaps it was always a place destined to have a swamp.
Beautiful dam, especially when it is up to the top of the water, in possibly one of the most beautiful villages in Spain. Beautiful views!
